So, the material with the largest Young's modulus will be the line with the smallest slope.

  • 1. Material A has the highest slope ŌåÆ smallest Y.

  • 2. Material C has the lowest slope ŌåÆ largest Y.

Therefore, material C has the largest Young's modulus. Hence, the correct option is (D).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Evening Shift
question:
A cubical block of density $\rho_b=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ floats in a liquid of density $\rho_{\mathrm{e}}=900 \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. If the height of block is $H=8.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ then height of the submerged part is

$\_\_\_\_$ cm .
solution:
When an object floats in a liquid, the weight of the object is exactly balanced by the buoyant force (up thrust) exerted by the liquid.

Weight of block is $=\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{m} \cdot \mathrm{g}=\left(\rho_{\mathrm{b}} \times \mathrm{V}_{\text {total }}\right) \times \mathrm{g}$

Buoyant force ( $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}$ ) acting on a submerged body is equal to the weight of displaced liquid,

$$ F_B=\left(\rho_1 V_{\text {submerged }}\right) \times g $$

For flotation :

$$ \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}} $$

$$ \rho_{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{~V}_{\text {total }} \mathrm{g}=\rho_{\mathrm{l}} \mathrm{~V}_{\text {submerged }} \mathrm{g} $$

For a cubical block with a uniform cross-sectional area (A):

Total Volume $=\mathrm{V}_{\text {total }}=\mathrm{AH}$

Submerged Volume $\mathrm{V}_{\text {submerged }}=\mathrm{Ah}$ (where h is the submerged height)

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \rho_{\mathrm{b}}(\mathrm{AH})=\rho_{\mathrm{l}}(\mathrm{Ah}) \Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=\left(\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{b}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{l}}}\right) \mathrm{H} $$

Putting the given values,

$$ \rho_{\mathrm{b}}=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3, \rho_{\mathrm{l}}=900 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3 \text { and } \mathrm{H}=8.0 \mathrm{~cm} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{~h}=\left(\frac{600}{900}\right) \times 8.0 \mathrm{~cm} \approx 5.33 \mathrm{~cm} $$

Therefore, the height of the submerged part of the block is approximately 5.3 cm.

Hence, the correct option is (D).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Evening Shift
question:
A soap bubble of surface tension $0.04 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is blown to a diameter of 7 cm . If $(15000-x) \mu \mathrm{J}$ of work is done in blowing it further to make its diameter 14 cm , then the value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .

$$ (\pi=22 / 7) $$
solution:
A soap bubble has two surfaces (inner and outer). The total surface energy $(U)$ is given by :

$$ U=2 \times S \times A=2 \times S \times\left(4 \pi R^2\right) $$

where $S$ is the surface tension and $A$ is the surface area.

The work done ( $W$ ) to increase the size is equal to the change in surface energy :

$$ \mathrm{W}=\Delta \mathrm{U}=8 \pi \mathrm{~S}\left(\mathrm{R}_2^2-\mathrm{R}_1^2\right) $$

$$ \mathrm{W}=8 \pi \mathrm{~S}\left[\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}_2}{2}\right)^2-\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}_1}{2}\right)^2\right]=2 \pi \mathrm{~S}\left(\mathrm{D}_2^2-\mathrm{D}_1^2\right) $$

The given values are,

Surface tension $\mathrm{N}=0.04 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$

Initial diameter $D_1=7 \mathrm{~cm}=0.07 \mathrm{~m}$

Final diameter $\mathrm{D}_2=14 \mathrm{~cm}=0.14 \mathrm{~m}$

So, the work done to increase the size of bubble is,

$$ \mathrm{W}=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 0.04 \times\left[(0.14)^2-(0.07)^2\right] $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=\frac{44}{7} \times 0.04 \times[0.0196-0.0049]$

$$ \mathrm{W}=0.003696 \mathrm{~J} $$

Now, as we know $1 \mathrm{~J}=10^6 \mu \mathrm{~J}$,

$$ \mathrm{W}=0.003696 \times 10^6 \mu \mathrm{~J}=3696 \mu \mathrm{~J} $$

$\Rightarrow 15000-x=3696$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $x=15000-3696$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{x}=11304 $$

Therefore, the value of x is 11304.

Hence, the correct answer is $\mathbf{1 1 3 0 4}$.

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Morning Shift
question: A brass wire of length 2 m and radius 1 mm at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is held taut between two rigid supports. Initially it was cooled to a temperature of $-43^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ creating a tension $T$ in the wire. The temperature to which the wire has to be cooled in order to increase the tension in it to $1.4 T$, is $\_\_\_\_$ ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
solution:
A metal rod of original length $L$ that is free to move. If we heat this rod by a temperature difference $\Delta T$, it will naturally expand.

The change in length ( $\Delta \mathrm{L}$ ) is given by the law of linear thermal expansion :

$$ \Delta \mathrm{L}=\mathrm{L} \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta \mathrm{~T} $$

Where $\alpha=$ Coefficient of Linear Expansion

When a wire fixed at both ends is cooled, it contracts. Since the supports are rigid, they prevent this contraction, creating a tension in the wire.

$$ \text { Strain } \varepsilon=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{~L}}=\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T} $$

According to Hooke's Law, Stress is proportional to Strain within the elastic limit. The constant of proportionality is Young's Modulus (Y).

$$ Y=\frac{\text { Stress }}{\text { Strain }} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ Stress $(\sigma)=\mathrm{Y} \times$ Strain

$$ \sigma=\mathrm{Y} \cdot(\alpha \Delta \mathrm{~T}) $$

Stress is defined as Force (F) per unit Area (A).

$$ \text { Stress }=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{~A}}=\mathrm{Y} \cdot(\alpha \Delta \mathrm{~T}) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{F}=(\mathrm{Y} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}) \times \mathrm{A}$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{F} \propto \Delta \mathrm{~T} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~F}_1}{\mathrm{~F}_2}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_1}{\Delta \mathrm{~T}_2} $$

Initial Temperature $=\mathrm{t}_0=27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Final Temperature $=\mathrm{t}_1=-43^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$$ \Delta \mathrm{T}_1=27-(-43)=70^{\circ} \mathrm{C} $$

Initial tension in the wire is, $\mathrm{F}_1=\mathrm{T}$

Final tension in the wire is $\mathrm{F}_2=1.4 \mathrm{~T}$

Let the new final temperature be $t_2$.

$$ \Delta \mathrm{T}_2=27-\mathrm{t}_2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{\mathrm{F}_2}{\mathrm{~F}_1}=\frac{1.4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{~T}}=\frac{27-\mathrm{t}_2}{70}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $1.4=\frac{27-t_2}{70}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $27-t_2=1.4 \times 70$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $27-t_2=98$

$$\Rightarrow $$$ t_2=27-98 $

$$\Rightarrow $$$ t_2=-71^{\circ} \mathrm{C} $

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Morning Shift
question:
Sixty four rain drops of radius 1 mm each falling down with a terminal velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ coalesce to form a bigger drop. The terminal velocity of bigger drop is

$\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
When a spherical drop falls through a viscous medium (like air), it reaches a constant speed called terminal velocity $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{t}}\right)$ when the net force on it is zero.

The forces involved are :

Weight ( W ) is acting downwards, $\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{mg}=$ Volume $\times \rho \times \mathrm{g}$.

Buoyant force ( $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}$ ) is acting upwards.

Viscous drag force ( $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{v}}$ ) is acting upwards

According to Stokes' Law $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{v}}=6 \pi \eta \mathrm{r}$ v.

At equilibrium, the net force must be zero.

$$ \mathrm{W}-\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{v}} $$

This leads to the standard formula for terminal velocity :

$$ v_t=\frac{2 r^2 g(\rho-\sigma)}{9 \eta} $$

Where :

  • $\mathrm{r}=$ Radius of the drop

  • $\rho=$ Density of the drop (water)

  • $\sigma=$ Density of the medium (air)

  • $\eta=$ Coefficient of viscosity

  • $\mathrm{g}=$ Acceleration due to gravity

Since $\mathrm{g}, \rho, \sigma$, and $\eta$ are constants for both the small and big drops, the terminal velocity is directly proportional to the square of the radius.

$$ v_t \propto r^2 $$

When 64 small drops combine to form one big drop, the total volume remains conserved.

  • Let r be the radius of a small drop.

  • Let R be the radius of the big drop.

  • Let $\mathrm{n}=64$ be the number of drops.

Volume of Big Drop $=\mathrm{n} \times$ (Volume of Small Drop)

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^3=\mathrm{n} \times \frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{R}^3=\mathrm{n} \cdot \mathrm{r}^3$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{n}^{1 / 3} \cdot \mathrm{r} $$

Substituting $\mathrm{n}=64$ :

$$ \mathrm{R}=64^{1 / 3} \cdot \mathrm{r} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=4 \mathrm{r} $$

So, the ratio of the terminal velocities for big and small drop using the fact that terminal velocity is proportional to square of radius,

$$ \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\text {big }}}{\mathrm{v}_{\text {small }}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}^2}{\mathrm{r}^2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{r}}\right)^2=\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{r}}{4}\right)^2=16 $$

Given $\mathrm{v}_{\text {small }}=10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$.

$$ v_{\text {big }}=16 \times v_{\text {small }} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $v_{\mathrm{big}}=16 \times 10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{big}}=160 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Morning Shift
question: Two wires $A$ and $B$ made of different materials of lengths 6.0 cm and 5.4 cm , respectively and area of cross sections $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^2$ and $4.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^2$, respectively are stretched by the same magnitude under a given load. The ratio of the Young's modulus of $A$ to that of $B$ is $x: 3$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
Young's Modulus is given by :

$$ \mathrm{Y}=\frac{\text { Stress }}{\text { Strain }}=\frac{\mathrm{F} / \mathrm{A}}{\Delta \mathrm{~L} / \mathrm{L}}=\frac{\mathrm{FL}}{\mathrm{~A} \Delta \mathrm{~L}} $$

Where:

  • F is the applied force (load)

  • L is the original length.

  • A is the area of cross-section

  • $\quad \Delta \mathrm{L}$ is the change in length (stretching)

According to question, both wires are stretched by the same magnitude ( $\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}$ ) under the same given load $\left(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}\right)$.

Therefore, for both wires:

$$ Y \propto \frac{L}{A} $$

Let $Y_A$ and $Y_B$ be the Young's moduli for wires $A$ and $B$ :

$$ \frac{Y_A}{Y_B}=\frac{\left(\frac{L_A}{A_A}\right)}{\left(\frac{L_B}{A_B}\right)}=\frac{L_A}{L_B} \times \frac{A_B}{A_A} $$

The given values :

  • $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.0 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{B}}=5.4 \mathrm{~cm}$

  • $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}}=3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^2, \mathrm{~A}_{\mathrm{B}}=4.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^2$

$$ \begin{gathered} \frac{Y_A}{Y_B}=\frac{6.0}{5.4} \times \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} \\ \frac{Y_A}{Y_B}=\frac{5}{3}=\frac{x}{3} \Rightarrow x=5 \end{gathered} $$

So, the value of x is 5 . Hence, the correct option is (D).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift
question: The surface tension of a soap bubble is 0.03 N/m. The work done in increasing the diameter of bubble from 2 cm to 6 cm is $\alpha \pi \times 10^{-4}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _________. (Take $\pi = 3.14$)
solution:
Work done in increasing the size of a soap bubble is stored as surface energy.

A soap bubble has two free surfaces (the inner and the outer air-liquid interfaces). The work done W is given by :

$$ \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{T} \times(2 \times \Delta \mathrm{A}) $$

Where T is surface tension and $\Delta \mathrm{A}$ is the change in area of one surface

$$ \Delta A=\left(4 \pi R_2^2-4 \pi R_1^2\right) $$

Putting the expression in work done,

$$ \mathrm{W}=2 \mathrm{~T} \times 4 \pi\left(\mathrm{R}_2^2-\mathrm{R}_1^2\right)=8 \pi \mathrm{~T}\left(\mathrm{R}_2^2-\mathrm{R}_1^2\right) $$

The surface tension of soap bubble is $\mathrm{T}=0.03 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$

The initial diameter is $\mathrm{D}_1=2 \mathrm{~cm} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_1=1 \mathrm{~cm}=1 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$

The final diameter is $\mathrm{D}_2=6 \mathrm{~cm} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_2=3 \mathrm{~cm}=3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$

Substitute the values into the expression for work done :

$$ \mathrm{W}=8 \pi(0.03)\left[\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2-\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2\right] $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=0.24 \pi\left[9 \times 10^{-4}-1 \times 10^{-4}\right]$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=0.24 \pi\left[8 \times 10^{-4}\right]$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=1.92 \pi \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J}=\alpha \pi \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J}$

$$ \Rightarrow \alpha=1.92 $$

Therefore, the value of $\alpha$ is 1.92 .

Hence, the correct option is (C).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift
question: If an air bubble of diameter 2 mm rises steadily through a liquid of density 2000 kg/m3 at a rate of 0.5 cm/s, then the coefficient of viscosity of liquid is ______ Poise. (Take g = 10 m/s2)
solution:
When the bubble rises steadily, the net force is zero. The forces involved are :

Buoyant force $\left(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}\right)$ acting upwards: $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{V} \sigma \mathrm{g}$, where $\sigma$ is liquid density and V is the volume of the bubble.

Weight ( $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{g}}$ ) acting downwards: $\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{V} \rho \mathrm{g}$, where $\rho$ is air density.

Viscous drag $\left(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{v}}\right)$ acting downwards (opposing motion) :

$\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{v}}=6 \pi \eta \mathrm{rv}$, where $\eta$ is the viscosity of liquid, r is the radius of body and v is the speed.

As the bubble is rising steadily, so the net force acting on it should be zero.

Equating the forces :

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{W}+\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{v}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ V \sigma \mathrm{~g}=V \rho \mathrm{~g}+6 \pi \eta \mathrm{rv} $$

Since the density of air ( $\rho$ ) is much smaller than the density of the liquid ( $\sigma=2000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ), we can neglect W :

$$ \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \sigma g=6 \pi \eta r v $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \eta=\frac{2 r^2 \sigma g}{9 v} $$

The radius of bubble is $\mathrm{r}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~mm}}{2}=1 \mathrm{~mm}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$

The density of liquid is $\sigma=2000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$

The constant speed of rise of bubble is $\mathrm{v}=0.5 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}=0.005 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}=5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

And the acceleration due to gravity is $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

Putting the values,

$$ \eta=\frac{2 \times\left(10^{-3}\right)^2 \times 2000 \times 10}{9 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\eta=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^4}{45 \times 10^{-3}}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-2}}{45 \times 10^{-3}}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \eta=\frac{40}{45} \approx 0.888 \mathrm{~Pa} \cdot \mathrm{~s} $$

As Pa.s is the SI unit of viscosity and the required value is in CGS unit, Poise;

$$ 1 \mathrm{~Pa} \cdot \mathrm{~s}=10 \text { Poise } \Rightarrow \eta=0.888 \times 10=8.88 \text { Poise } $$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift
question: A liquid drop of diameter 2 mm breaks into 512 droplets. The change in surface energy is $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Take surface tension of liquid = 0.08 N/m)
solution:
Let $R$ be the radius of the big drop and $r$ be the radius of each small droplet. Since volume is conserved during the process:

Volume of big drop $= 512 \times$ Volume of one small droplet

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = 512 \times \left(\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3\right)$$

$$R^3 = 512\,r^3$$

$$R = (512)^{1/3}\,r = 8r$$

$$r = \frac{R}{8}$$

The diameter of bigger drop is $D = 2\ \mathrm{mm}$, so $R = 1\ \mathrm{mm} = 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m}$.

Thus, $r = \frac{10^{-3}}{8}\ \mathrm{m}$.

Surface energy is defined as surface tension $(T) \times (A)$.

Initial Surface Energy $U_i = T \times (4\pi R^2)$

Final Surface Energy $U_f = 512 \times T \times (4\pi r^2)$

$$\Delta U = U_f - U_i = 4\pi T\left(512\,r^2 - R^2\right)$$

Substituting $r = \frac{R}{8}$:

$$\Delta U = 4\pi T\left(512\left(\frac{R}{8}\right)^2 - R^2\right) = 4\pi T\left(\frac{512R^2}{64} - R^2\right)$$

$$\Delta U = 4\pi T(8R^2 - R^2) = 4\pi T(7R^2)$$

$$\Delta U = 28\pi T R^2$$

The surface tension of liquid is $T = 0.08\ \mathrm{N/m}$ and $R = 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m}$:

Putting the values,

$$\Delta U = 28 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 0.08 \times (10^{-3})^2$$

$$\Delta U = 7.04 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{J} = \alpha \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{J}$$

$$\alpha \approx 7$$

The value of $\alpha$ is $7$. Therefore, the correct option is (B).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift
question: A uniform wire of length $l$ of weight $w$ is suspended from the roof with a weight of $W$ at the other end. The stress in the wire at $\frac{l}{3}$ distance from the top is $\left( \frac{W}{A} + \frac{2}{\gamma} \frac{w}{A} \right)$, where $A$ is the cross sectional area of the wire. The value of $\gamma$ is ________.
solution:
Stress is force (tension) per unit cross-sectional area.

In a suspended wire with mass, the tension varies along its length because the wire must support the weight of the portion below it.

We need the stress at a distance $\mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{3}$ from the top. This means the point is $\mathrm{l}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{21}{3}$ distance from the bottom.

The total tension $T$ at this point must support the load weight $W$ attached to the bottom and weight of the portion

of the wire hanging below this point $(w')$.

Since the wire is uniform, weight per unit length is $\lambda = \frac{w}{l}$.

The length of the wire below the point is $L_{\text{below}} = \frac{2l}{3}$.

$$w' = \lambda \times L_{\text{below}} = \left(\frac{W}{l}\right) \left(\frac{2l}{3}\right) = \frac{2w}{3}$$

So, total tension $T$ at the point:

$$T = W + w' = W + \frac{2w}{3}$$

So, the stress in wire is,

$$\sigma = \frac{T}{A} = \frac{W + \frac{2w}{3}}{A}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{W}{A} + \frac{2w}{3A} = \left(\frac{W}{A} + \frac{2w}{\gamma A}\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{2}{3} = \frac{2}{\gamma}$$

$$\gamma = 3$$

Therefore, the value of $\gamma$ is $3$. Thus, the correct answer is $3$.

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift
question:
A tub is filled with water and a wooden cube $10\ \text{cm} \times 10\ \text{cm} \times 10\ \text{cm}$ is placed in the water. The wooden cube is found to float on the water with a part of it submerged in water. When a metal coin is placed on the wooden cube, the submerged part is increased by $3.87$ cm. The mass of the metal coin is ________ gram.

(Take water density as $1\ \text{g/cm}^3$ and density of wood as $0.4\ \text{g/cm}^3$)
solution:
When the wooden cube floats by itself, it is in equilibrium. The upward buoyant force ( $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b} 1}$ ) equals the downward weight of the wood ( $\mathrm{W}_{\text {wood }}$ ).

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b} 1}=\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{wood}} $$

The buoyant force is given by the weight of the displaced water :

$$ \rho_{\text {water }} \cdot V_{\text {submerged1 }} \cdot g=m_{\text {wood }} \cdot g $$

When a metal coin of mass $\mathrm{m}_{\text {coin }}$ is placed on top of the cube, the total weight increases. To remain in equilibrium, the cube must displace more water to provide an additional buoyant force ( $\mathrm{F}_{\text {extra }}$ ) equal to the weight of the coin.

Total Weight $=\mathrm{W}_{\text {wood }}+\mathrm{W}_{\text {coin }}$

Total Buoyant Force $=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b} 1}+\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{extra}}$

Since $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b} 1}=\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{wood}}$,

$$F_{\text{extra}} = W_{\text{coin}}$$

The extra buoyant force is equal to the weight of the additional volume of water displaced due to the coin.

Let $A$ be the surface area of the cube and $\Delta h$ be the increase in submerged depth.

So, the additional volume displaced is,

$$\Delta V = A \cdot \Delta h$$

$$F_{\text{extra}} = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot \Delta V \cdot g = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot (A \cdot \Delta h) \cdot g$$

$$m_{\text{coin}} \cdot g = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot (A \cdot \Delta h) \cdot g$$

$$m_{\text{coin}} = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot A \cdot \Delta h$$

The density of water is $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1\ \mathrm{g/cm^3}$

The side of the cube is $s = 10\ \mathrm{cm}$

So, the surface area is $A = s \times s = 10\ \mathrm{cm} \times 10\ \mathrm{cm} = 100\ \mathrm{cm^2}$

The increase in submerged depth, $\Delta h = 3.87\ \mathrm{cm}$

As all the given values are in CGS units so the calculated mass will be in grams.

$$m_{\text{coin}} = 1 \times 100 \times 3.87\mathrm{g}$$

$$m_{\text{coin}} = 387\mathrm{g}$$

Therefore, the mass of the metal coin is 387 grams. Thus, the correct answer is 387.

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift
question: A water spray gun is attached to a hose of cross sectional area $30 \mathrm{~cm}^2$. The gun comprises of 10 perforations each of cross sectional area of $15 \mathrm{~mm}^2$. If the water flows in the hose with the speed of $50 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$, calculate the speed at which the water flows out from each perforation. (Neglect any edge effects)
solution:
For an incompressible fluid, the volume of fluid entering a pipe per unit time must equal the volume of fluid leaving the pipe per unit time.

Volume Flow Rate (In) = Volume Flow Rate (Out)

$$ A_1 v_1=A_2 v_2 $$

Where A is the area and v is the velocity of the fluid.

The area of hose is $\mathrm{A}_1=30 \mathrm{~cm}^2=30 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$.

The speed in hose is $\mathrm{v}_1=50 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

Number of perforations is $\mathrm{n}=10$.

The area of one perforation is $\mathrm{a}=15 \mathrm{~mm}^2=15 \times\left(10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^2=15 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2$

So, total area of exit is, $\mathrm{A}_2=\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{a}=10 \times 15 \times 10^{-6}=150 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2$

Using the equation of continuity:

$$ A_1 v_1=A_2 v_2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ v_2=\frac{A_1 v_1}{A_2} $$

Substituting the values :

$$ v_2=\frac{\left(30 \times 10^{-4}\right) \times 0.5}{150 \times 10^{-6}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $v_2=\frac{15 \times 10^{-4}}{150 \times 10^{-6}}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{v}_2=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

Therefore, the speed at which the water flows out from each perforation is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

Thus, the correct option is (B).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift
question: A metal string $A$ is suspended from a rigid support and its free end is attached to a block of mass $M$. Second block having mass 2 M is suspended at the bottom of the first block using a string $B$. The area of cross sections of strings $A$ and $B$ are same. The ratio of lengths of strings of $A$ to B is 2 and the ratio of their Young's moduli $\left(Y_A / Y_B\right)$ is 0.5 . The ratio of elongations in $A$ to $B$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
solution:
Young's Modulus (Y) is defined as the ratio of tensile stress to tensile strain :

$$ \mathrm{Y}=\frac{\text { Stress }}{\text { Strain }}=\frac{\mathrm{F} / \mathrm{A}}{\Delta \mathrm{~L} / \mathrm{L}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{FL}}{\mathrm{AY}} \ldots (ii)$$

Where, F is the tension in the string, L is the original length, A is the area of cross-section. Y is the Young's Modulus.

For string B

This string supports only the bottom block of mass 2 M .

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{Mg} $$

For string A

This string supports both mass M and the mass 2 M hanging below it.

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{Mg}+2 \mathrm{Mg}=3 \mathrm{Mg} $$

The ratio of lengths is $\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=2$

The ratio of Young's moduli is $\frac{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}=0.5$.

The areas are same, $A_A=A_B=A$.

Using the formula of extension in string (i),

$$ \frac{\Delta L_A}{\Delta L_B}=\frac{F_A L_A / A_A Y_A}{F_B L_B / A_B Y_B}=\left(\frac{F_A}{F_B}\right) \times\left(\frac{L_A}{L_B}\right)\left(\frac{Y_B}{Y_A}\right) $$

Substituting the values :

$$ \frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(\frac{3 \mathrm{Mg}}{2 \mathrm{Mg}}\right) \times(2) \times\left(\frac{1}{0.5}\right) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \times 2 \times 2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=3 \times 2=6 $$

Therefore, the ratio of elongations in A to B is 6.

Thus, the correct option is (D).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
question:
A string $A$ of length 0.314 m and Young's modulus $2 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$ is connected to another string $B$ of length and Young's modulus both twice of those of $A$. This series combination of strings is then suspended from a rigid support and its free end is fixed to a load of mass 0.8 kg . The net change in length of the combination is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.

(radius of both the strings is 0.2 mm and acceleration due to gravity $=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ) (Mass of both strings is to be neglected as compared to the mass of load)
solution:
Young's Modulus is defined as the ratio of longitudinal stress $(\sigma)$ to longitudinal strain $(\epsilon)$ :

$$ \mathrm{Y}=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon}=\frac{\mathrm{F} / \mathrm{A}}{\Delta \mathrm{~L} / \mathrm{L}} $$

$$ \Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{FL}}{\mathrm{AY}} $$

Where :

  • F is the tension in the string ( $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{mg}$ for a suspended load).

  • L is the original length.

  • A is the cross-sectional area ( $\pi r^2$ ).

  • Y is the Young's Modulus.

For String A

The length of string is $L_A=0.314 \mathrm{~m} \approx \frac{\pi}{10} \mathrm{~m}$

Young's modulus is $Y_A=2 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$

The radius of wire is $\mathrm{r}=0.2 \mathrm{~mm}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}$

So, the area of string A is $\mathrm{A}=\pi\left(2 \times 10^{-4}\right)^2=4 \pi \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~m}^2$

The tensile force is $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{mg}=0.8 \times 10=8 \mathrm{~N}$

So, the elongation in string A is,

$$ \Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{8 \times 0.314}{\left(4 \pi \times 10^{-8}\right) \times\left(2 \times 10^{10}\right)} $$

Using $0.314 \approx \frac{\pi}{10}$ :

$$ \Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{8 \times\left(\frac{\pi}{10}\right)}{8 \pi \times 10^2}=\frac{\pi}{10 \times \pi \times 10^2}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}=1 \mathrm{~mm} $$

For string B

It is given that $L_B=2 L_A$ and $Y_B=2 Y_A$, while $F$ and $A$ remain the same:

$$ \Delta L_B=\frac{F\left(2 L_A\right)}{A\left(2 Y_A\right)}=\frac{F L_A}{A Y_A}=\Delta L_A $$

So, $\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}=1 \mathrm{~mm}$.

Hence, the total elongation of the combination is,

$$ \Delta \mathrm{L}_{\text {total }}=\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}+\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}=1 \mathrm{~mm}+1 \mathrm{~mm}=2 \mathrm{~mm} $$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
question:
The increase in the pressure required to decrease the volume ( $\Delta V$ ) of water is $6.3 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The percentage decrease in the volume is $\_\_\_\_$ .

(Bulk modulus of water $=2.1 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$.)
solution:
When a uniform pressure (or stress) is applied to the surface of a fluid, it undergoes a decrease in volume.

The bulk modulus ( $\beta$ ) is defined as the ratio of the volumetric stress (change in pressure, $\Delta \mathrm{P}$ ) to the volumetric strain (fractional change in volume, $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}$ ).

$$ \beta=\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta V / V} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{~V}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\beta} $$

Increase in pressure is $\Delta \mathrm{P}=6.3 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$

Bulk Modulus of water is $\beta=2.1 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$

Substituting the given values,

$$ \frac{\Delta V}{V}=\frac{6.3 \times 10^7}{2.1 \times 10^9} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{~V}}=3 \times 10^{-2} $$

The percentage decrease in volume is $\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\right) \times 100$

$$ \Delta \%=\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right) \times 100=3 \% $$

Therefore, the percentage decrease in the volume is $3 \%$.

Thus, the correct option is (B).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
question:
The surface tension of a soap solution is $3.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. The work required to increase the radius of a soap bubble from 1 cm to 2 cm is $\alpha \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .

$$ (\pi=22 / 7) $$
solution:
A soap bubble has two free surfaces (an inner surface and an outer surface). The surface energy (U) of a bubble with radius r and surface tension T is:

$$ \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{T} \times \text { (Total Surface Area) } $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ U=T \times\left(2 \times 4 \pi r^2\right)=8 \pi r^2 T $$

The work done $(W)$ in increasing the radius from $r_1$ to $r_2$ is equal to the increase in surface energy:

$$ \mathrm{W}=\Delta \mathrm{U}=8 \pi \mathrm{~T}\left(\mathrm{r}_2^2-\mathrm{r}_1^2\right) $$

The surface tension of soap solution is given as $\mathrm{T}=3.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$

The initial radius of soap bubble is $\mathrm{r}_1=1 \mathrm{~cm}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$

The final radius of soap bubble is $\mathrm{r}_2=2 \mathrm{~cm}=2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$

And the value of $\pi=22 / 7$

Putting the values,

$$ \mathrm{W}=8 \times \frac{22}{7} \times\left(3.5 \times 10^{-2}\right) \times\left[\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2-\left(10^{-2}\right)^2\right] $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $W=8 \times 22 \times\left(0.5 \times 10^{-2}\right) \times\left[4 \times 10^{-4}-1 \times 10^{-4}\right]$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $W=176 \times 0.005 \times\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $W=0.88 \times 3 \times 10^{-4}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=2.64 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=264 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}=\alpha \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}$

$$ \Rightarrow \alpha=264 $$

Therefore, the value of $\alpha$ is 264 .

Thus, the correct answer is 264 .

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
question: Eight mercury drops, each of radius $r$, coalesce to form a bigger drop. The surface energy released in this process is $\_\_\_\_$ - ( $S$ is the surface tension of mercury).
options:
$8 \pi r^2 \mathrm{~S}$

$16 \pi r^2 S$

$64 \pi r^2 S$

$4 \pi r^2 \mathrm{~S}$
solution:
When 8 small drops of radius $r$ combine to form one large drop of radius $R$, the total volume remains constant.

Volume of 8 small drops $=$ Volume of 1 large drop

$$ 8 \times\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3\right)=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $8 r^3=R^3$

$$ \Rightarrow R=2 r $$

The surface energy of a single drop is $E=S A$ where $S$ is the surface tension and $A$ is the surface area.

Total initial area for 8 drops $=8 \times\left(4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2\right)=32 \pi \mathrm{r}^2$.

So, total initial surface energy is $\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=32 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{~S}$

The final drop has radius $\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{r}$.

So, final surface area $=4 \pi R^2=4 \pi(2 r)^2=4 \pi\left(4 r^2\right)=16 \pi r^2$.

So, the final surface energy is $U_f=16 \pi r^2 S$

The energy released is the decrease in surface energy :

$$ \Delta U=U_i-U_f $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\Delta U=32 \pi r^2 S-16 \pi r^2 S$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \Delta U=16 \pi r^2 S $$

Therefore, the surface energy released in this process is $16 \pi r^2 S$.

Hence, the correct option is (B).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
question:
A copper wire of length 3 m is stretched by 3 mm by applying an external force. The volume of the wire is $600 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^3$. The elastic potential energy stored in the wire in stretched condition would be

$\_\_\_\_$ J.

(Given Young modulus of copper $=1.1 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$ )
solution:
Elastic potential energy $(U)$ is the work done in stretching a wire. For a wire with Young's modulus $Y$, length $L$, and cross-sectional area A , the force F required for an extension x is:

$$ F=\frac{Y A x}{L} $$

Work done (dW) for a small extension dx:

$$ \mathrm{dW}=\mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{dx}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{YA}}{\mathrm{~L}}\right) \mathrm{xdx} $$

To find the total work done in stretching the wire to $\Delta \mathrm{L}$ we need to integrate from 0 to total extension $\Delta \mathrm{L}$ :

$$ \mathrm{U}=\int_0^{\Delta \mathrm{L}} \frac{\mathrm{YAL}}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{\mathrm{YA}}{2 \mathrm{~L}}(\Delta \mathrm{~L})^2 $$

Since volume $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{L}$, we can substitute $\mathrm{A}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{L}}$ :

$$ \begin{gathered} \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Y}\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{~L}}{\mathrm{~L}}\right)^2 \times \mathrm{V} \\ \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \times \text { Young's Modulus } \times(\text { Strain })^2 \times \text { Volume } \end{gathered} $$

The length of wire is $\mathrm{L}=3 \mathrm{~m}$

The elongation in the length of wire is $\Delta \mathrm{L}=3 \mathrm{~mm}=3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$

The volume of wire is $\mathrm{V}=600 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^3$

The Young's modulus of copper is $\mathrm{Y}=1.1 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$

The strain in wire due to elongation is

$$ \epsilon=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{~L}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{3}=10^{-3} $$

Now, substituting into the energy formula:

$$ U=\frac{1}{2} \times\left(1.1 \times 10^{11}\right) \times\left(10^{-3}\right)^2 \times\left(600 \times 10^{-6}\right) $$

$\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \times 1.1 \times 10^{11} \times 10^{-6} \times 600 \times 10^{-6}$

$$ \begin{gathered} U=0.5 \times 1.1 \times 600 \times 10^{11-6-6} \\ U=0.5 \times 1.1 \times 600 \times 10^{-1} \\ U=33 \mathrm{~J} \end{gathered} $$

Therefore, the elastic potential energy stored is 33 J .

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift
question: Two wires as shown in the figure below, made of steel and have breaking stress of $12 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. Area of cross-section of upper wire is $0.008 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and of lower wire is $0.004 \mathrm{~cm}^2$. The maximum mass that can be added to pan without breaking any wire is $\_\_\_\_$ kg.
solution:
Breaking tension ( $\mathrm{T}_{\max }$ ) is the maximum tension that can wire withstand without breaking.

If the breaking stress of wire is $\sigma_{\max }$ and area of cross-section is $\mathrm{A}, \sigma_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\max }}{\mathrm{A}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_{\max }=\sigma_{\max } \times \mathrm{A}$

For the lower wire the area of cross-section is $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{L}}=0.004 \mathrm{~cm}^2=4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}^2$ :

$$ \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{L}(\max )}=\left(12 \times 10^8\right) \times\left(4 \times 10^{-7}\right)=480 \mathrm{~N} $$

For the upper wire the area of cross-section is $A_U=0.008 \mathrm{~cm}^2=8 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}^2$ :

$$ \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{U}(\max )}=\left(12 \times 10^8\right) \times\left(8 \times 10^{-7}\right)=960 \mathrm{~N} $$

Let M be the mass added to the pan.

As lower wire supports the pan and the 10 kg mass.

$$ T_L=(M+10) g=(M+10) \times 10 $$

For lower wire not to break:

$$10(M+10) \leq 480 \Rightarrow M+10 \leq 48 \Rightarrow M \leq 38 \mathrm{~kg}.....(ii)$$

The upper wire supports the pan, 10 kg mass, and 30 kg mass.

$$ \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{U}}=(\mathrm{M}+10+30) \mathrm{g}=(\mathrm{M}+40) \times 10 $$

For upper wire not to break:

$$10(M+40) \leq 960 \Rightarrow M+40 \leq 96 \Rightarrow M \leq 56 \mathrm{~kg} \ldots (ii)$$

To ensure neither wire breaks, we must choose the smaller of the two values. If we add 56 kg , the lower wire will break. If we add 38 kg , both wires remain intact. Therefore, the maximum load that can be placed in pan is 38 kg .

Thus, the correct option is (B).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift
question:
The Young's modulus of steel wire of radius $r$ and length $L$ is $Y$.

If the radius $r$ and length $L$ of the wire are doubled then the value of $Y$
options:
increases by two times

reduces by half

remains unchanged

becomes one fourth
solution:
Young's Modulus is defined as the ratio of tensile stress $(\sigma)$ to tensile strain $(\epsilon)$ :

$$ \mathrm{Y}=\frac{\text { Stress }}{\text { Strain }}=\frac{\mathrm{F} / \mathrm{A}}{\Delta \mathrm{~L} / \mathrm{L}} $$

Young's Modulus is an intrinsic property of the material. It depends solely on the nature of the material and the temperature, not on the dimensions (length, area, or radius) of the specific sample.

The radius ( r ) and length ( L ) are doubled.

Doubling the radius changes the area $\left(A=\pi r^2\right)$, the area of cross-section becomes 4 times the initial and doubling the length changes the original length (L).

However, because the material remains steel, the inter-atomic forces and the elastic constant of the substance do not change.

Since Young's modulus (Y) is independent of dimensions, changing $L$ or $r$ will not affect the value of Y.

Hence, the correct option is (C).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift
question: A cube has side length 5 cm and modulus of rigidity $10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The displacement produced by a force of 10 N in the upper face of cube is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
solution:
The modulus of rigidity (or shear modulus) is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain.

$$ \eta=\frac{\text { Shear Stress }}{\text { Shear Strain }}=\frac{\mathrm{F} / \mathrm{A}}{\Delta \mathrm{x} / \mathrm{L}} $$

Where F is the tangential force applied, A is the area of the face, $\Delta \mathrm{x}$ is the relative displacement and L is the height (side length) of the cube.

The side length of cube is $\mathrm{L}=5 \mathrm{~cm}=0.05 \mathrm{~m}$

So, the area is $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{L}^2=(0.05)^2=0.0025 \mathrm{~m}^2=2.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2$

The magnitude of applied tangential force is $\mathrm{F}=10 \mathrm{~N}$

The shear modulus is $\eta=10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$

Using the formula of shear modulus;

$$ \eta=\frac{\mathrm{FL}}{\mathrm{~A} \Delta \mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{FL}}{\mathrm{~A} \eta} $$

Putting the values,

$\Delta \mathrm{x}=\frac{10 \times 0.05}{\left(2.5 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times 10^5}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\Delta x=\frac{0.5}{2.5 \times 10^2}=\frac{0.5}{250}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\Delta \mathrm{x}=0.002 \mathrm{~m}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \Delta \mathrm{x}=0.002 \times 1000 \mathrm{~mm}=2 \mathrm{~mm} $$

Therefore, the correct answer is 2 .

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift
question: Figure represents the extension $(\Delta l)$ of a wire of length 1 meter, suspended from the ceiling of the room at one end with a load $W$ connected to the other end. If the cross-sectional area of the wire is $10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^2$ then the Young's modulus of the wire is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}^2$.
solution:
Young's Modulus (Y) is defined as the ratio of tensile stress to tensile strain within the elastic limit.

Stress ( $\sigma$ ) is force (W) per unit cross-sectional area (A).

$$ \sigma=\frac{W}{A} $$

Strain $(\epsilon)$ is extension $(\Delta \mathrm{l})$ per unit original length $(\mathrm{L})$.

$$ \epsilon=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{~L}} $$

Thus, Young's Modulus is :

$$ Y=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon}=\frac{W / A}{\Delta l / L} $$

$$ \mathrm{Y}=\frac{\mathrm{WL}}{\mathrm{~A} \Delta \mathrm{l}} $$

The original length is $\mathrm{L}=1 \mathrm{~m}$

The cross-sectional area is $\mathrm{A}=10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^2$

From the graph, we need a pair of values for W and $\Delta \mathrm{l}$.

Let's take $\mathrm{W}=20 \mathrm{~N}$ and the corresponding extension $\Delta \mathrm{l}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}$.

Substituting the values in the expression for Young's modulus;

$$ Y=\frac{20 \times 1}{10^{-5} \times\left(2 \times 10^{-4}\right)} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $Y=\frac{20}{2 \times 10^{-9}}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $Y=10 \times 10^9$

$$ \mathrm{Y}=1.0 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2 $$

Therefore, the Young's modulus of the wire is $1.0 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. Thus, the correct option is (C).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift
question: A cylindrical vessel of 40 cm radius is completely filled with water and its capacity is $528 \mathrm{dm}^3$ (dm : decimeter) The vessel is placed on a solid block of exactly same height as vessel. If a small hole is made at 70 cm below the top of water level, then horizontal range of water falling on the ground in the beginning is $\_\_\_\_$ cm .
solution:
The capacity of vessel is $528 \mathrm{dm}^3$.

Since $1 \mathrm{dm}=10 \mathrm{~cm} \Rightarrow 1 \mathrm{dm}^3=1000 \mathrm{~cm}^3$

$$ \mathrm{V}=528,000 \mathrm{~cm}^3 $$

Volume of a cylinder is $\mathrm{V}=\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{H}$.

Given $\mathrm{r}=40 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$ 528,000=\times H $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{528,000}{\pi \times(40)^2}=\mathrm{H}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{H}=105 \mathrm{~cm} $$

Depth of hole from top is $\mathrm{h}=70 \mathrm{~cm}$.

From Torricelli's Law velocity of efflux is given as,

$$ \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gh}} $$

The vessel is on a block of height H and the hole is at distance $(\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{h})$ from the bottom of the vessel.

Total height from ground $\mathrm{Y}=(\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{h})+\mathrm{H}=2 \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{h}$

The time to fall is,

$$ t=\sqrt{\frac{2 Y}{g}} $$

The horizontal range of projectile is given as

$$ \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{v} \times \mathrm{t}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gh}} \times \sqrt{\frac{2(2 \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{h})}{\mathrm{g}}}=2 \sqrt{\mathrm{~h}(2 \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{h})} $$

The height of water level from the hole is $\mathrm{h}=70 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$ 2 \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{h}=2(105)-70=210-70=140 \mathrm{~cm} . $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{R}=2 \sqrt{70 \times 140}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $R=2 \sqrt{70 \times 70 \times 2}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=2 \times 70 \times \sqrt{2}=140 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm} $$

Therefore, the horizontal of water falling on the ground is $140 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$.

Thus. the correct option is (B).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift
question:
A lift of mass 1600 kg is supported by thick iron wire. If the maximum stress which the wire can withstand is $4 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$ and its radius is 4 mm , then maximum acceleration the lift can take is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$.

(take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and $\pi=3.14$ )
solution:
When a lift of mass M accelerates upward with acceleration a, the tension (T) in the supporting cable must overcome both gravity and provide the upward acceleration.

Using Newton's Second Law ( $\mathrm{F}_{\text {net }}=\mathrm{Ma}$ ):

$$ \mathrm{T}-\mathrm{Mg}=\mathrm{Ma} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~T}=\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{~g}+\mathrm{a}) $$

The stress $(\sigma)$ is defined as the restoring force (tension) per unit cross-sectional area (A):

$$ \sigma=\frac{T}{A}=\frac{M(g+a)}{A} $$

For a wire with a circular cross-section of radius r , the area of cross-section is $\mathrm{A}=\pi \mathrm{r}^2$.

$$ \sigma_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{M}\left(\mathrm{~g}+\mathrm{a}_{\max }\right)}{\pi \mathrm{r}^2} $$

The mass of lift is $\mathrm{M}=1600 \mathrm{~kg}$

The radius of cable is $\mathrm{r}=4 \mathrm{~mm}=4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$

So, the area is

$$ \mathrm{A}=\pi \mathrm{r}^2=3.14 \times\left(4 \times 10^{-3}\right)^2=3.14 \times 16 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{A}=50.24 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2 $$

So, the maximum stress is

$$ \sigma_{\max }=4 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2 $$

From the maximum stress formula,

$$ 4 \times 10^8=\frac{1600\left(10+\mathrm{a}_{\max }\right)}{50.24 \times 10^{-6}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4 \times 10^8 \times 50.24 \times 10^{-6}=1600\left(10+a_{\max }\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $200.96 \times 10^2=1600\left(10+a_{\max }\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $20096=1600\left(10+a_{\max }\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $10+a_{\max }=\frac{20096}{1600}=12.56$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{a}_{\max }=12.56-10=2.56 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 $$

Therefore, the maximum acceleration of the lift is $2.56 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$.

Thus, the correct option is (A).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift
question:
The two wires $A$ and $B$ of equal cross-section but of different materials are joined together. The ratio of Young's modulus of wire $A$ and wire $B$ is 20/11. When the joined wire is kept under certain tension the elongations in the wires $A$ and $B$ are equal. If the length of wire $A$ is 2.2 m , then the length of wire $B$ is

$\_\_\_\_$ m.
solution:
Young's Modulus is defined as the ratio of Stress $(\sigma)$ to Strain $(\epsilon)$.

$$ Y=\frac{\text { Stress }}{\text { Strain }}=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon} $$

Stress is force per unit area, $\sigma=\frac{\text { Force (F) }}{\text { Area (A) }}$

Strain is change in length per unit original length,

$$ \epsilon=\frac{\text { Change in length }(\Delta \mathrm{L})}{\text { Original length }(\mathrm{L})} $$

So, $Y=\frac{F / A}{\Delta L / L}$

$$ \Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{FL}}{\mathrm{AY}} $$

Since the wires are joined in series and kept under tension, the Force (F) acting on both wires is the same.

The area (A) is the same for both.

It is given that the elongation in both the wires is equal, $\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}$.

Since $\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}:$

$\frac{F L_A}{A Y_A}=\frac{F L_B}{A Y_B}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}} $$

The ratio of Young's modulus is $\frac{Y_A}{Y_B}=\frac{20}{11}$ and the length of wire $A$ is $L_A=2.2 \mathrm{~m}$

$\frac{2.2}{\mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{20}{11}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}=2.2 \times \frac{11}{20}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{24.2}{20}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}=1.21 \mathrm{~m} $$

Therefore, the length of wire B is 1.21 m . Hence, the correct option is (C).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift
question: A certain gas is isothermally compressed to $\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{\mathrm{rd}}$ of its initial volume $\left(V_{\mathrm{o}}=3\right.$ litre) by applying required pressure. If the bulk modulus of the gas is $3 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$, the magnitude of work done on the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
solution:
The Bulk Modulus of a material is defined as the ratio of the infinitesimal pressure increase (dP) to the resulting relative decrease of the volume:

$$ B=-\frac{d P}{d V / V}=-\frac{V d P}{d V} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{dP}=-\mathrm{B} \frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{~V}} . $$

The work done on a gas during a volume change is

$$ W=-\int\limits_{V_i}^{V_f} P d V $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $W=-\int\limits_{V_i}^{V_f} B \ln \left(\frac{V_i}{V}\right) d V$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{W}=\int \Delta \mathrm{P} \mathrm{dV} $$

However, for a gas where B is constant during isothermal compression, the work done is:

$\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{B} \Delta \mathrm{V}$ for small changes or $\mathrm{W}=\int \mathrm{P} \mathrm{dV}$

The initial volume is $V_i=3$ litres $=3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^3$

And the final volume is $V_f=13 \times 3=1$ litre $=1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^3$

So, the change in volume is;

$$ \Delta V=V_i-V_f=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^3 $$

The bulk modulus is $\mathrm{B}=3 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$

For an isothermal process in a gas, the Bulk Modulus is equal to the pressure ( $\mathrm{B}=\mathrm{P}$ ).

$$ W=\int_{V_i}^{V_f} P d V=\int_{V_i}^{V_f} B \frac{V_i}{V} d V $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{B} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}} \ln \left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}}\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=\left(3 \times 10^5\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times \ln \left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{\mathrm{~V}_0 / 3}\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ W=900 \times 1.0986 \approx 988.74 \mathrm{~J} $$

Therefore, the magnitude of work done on the gas is approximately 989 J .

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
question: A liquid of density $600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ flowing steadily in a tube of varying cross-section. The cross-section at a point $A$ is $1.0 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and that at $B$ is $20 \mathrm{~mm}^2$. Both the points $A$ and $B$ are in same horizontal plane, the speed of the liquid at $A$ is $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. The difference in pressures at $A$ and $B$ points is $\_\_\_\_$ Pa.
solution:
The density of liquid is $\rho=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$.

Area of cross-section at point A is $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}}=1.0 \mathrm{~cm}^2=1.0 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$.

Area of cross-section at point $B$ is $A_B=20.0 \mathrm{~mm}^2=20.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2=0.2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$.

The velocity at A is $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{A}}=10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}=0.1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

For an incompressible liquid in steady flow, the mass flow rate must remain constant.

Using equation of continuity,

$$ A_A v_A=A_B v_B $$

Substituting the values :

$$ \left(1.0 \times 10^{-4}\right) \times(0.1)=\left(0.2 \times 10^{-4}\right) \times v_B $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1.0 \times 0.1}{0.2}=\frac{0.1}{0.2}=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

According to Bernoulli's equation for a horizontal flow (where height $h$ is constant), the sum of pressure and kinetic energy per unit volume is constant :

$P_A+\frac{1}{2} \rho v_A^2=P_B+\frac{1}{2} \rho v_B^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ P_A-P_B=\Delta P_{A B}=\frac{1}{2} \rho\left(v_B^2-v_A^2\right) $$

Substituting the given values $\rho=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3, \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{A}}=0.1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

$$ P_A-P_B=\frac{1}{2} \times 600 \times\left(0.5^2-0.1^2\right) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $P_A-P_B=300 \times(0.25-0.01)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=300 \times 0.24$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=72 \mathrm{~Pa} $$

Therefore, the difference in pressures at points A and B is 72 Pa .

Hence, the correct option is (D).

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
question: A spherical liquid drop of radius $R$ acquires the terminal velocity $v_1$ when falls through a gas of viscosity $\eta$. Now the drop is broken into 64 identical droplets and each droplet acquires terminal velocity $v_2$ falling through the same gas. The ratio of terminal velocities $v_1 / v_2$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
solution:
When a spherical object falls through a viscous fluid, it eventually reaches a constant speed called terminal velocity $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{t}}\right)$. This occurs when the downward force of gravity is balanced by the upward buoyant force and the viscous drag force.

The force of gravity acting on the sphere is $F_g=m g=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho g$, where $\rho$ is the density of the liquid.

Using Archimedean's principle of buoyant force $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3 \sigma \mathrm{~g}$, where $\sigma$ is the density of the gas.

Using Stokes' Law the viscous drag is $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{v}}=6 \pi \eta \mathrm{r} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{t}}$

At terminal velocity,

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{g}}-\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $6 \pi \eta r v_t=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3(\rho-\sigma) g$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{r}^2(\rho-\sigma) \mathrm{g}}{9 \eta}$

For the same liquid and gas, terminal velocity is proportional to the square of the radius :

$$ \mathrm{v} \propto \mathrm{r}^2 $$

The large drop of radius $R$ is broken into 64 identical droplets of radius $r$. Since the total volume remains constant :

$$ V_R=64 \times V_r $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3=64 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{R}^3=64 \mathrm{r}^3$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ R=4 r \Rightarrow r=\frac{R}{4} $$

Let $\mathrm{v}_1$ be the terminal velocity of the large drop and $\mathrm{v}_2$ be the terminal velocity of a small droplet. Using the relationship $\mathrm{v} \propto \mathrm{r}^2$ :

$$ \frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{R}^2}{\mathrm{r}^2} $$

Substituting $\mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{4}$ :

$$ \frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{R}^2}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{4}\right)^2} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{v_1}{v_2}=\frac{R^2}{R^2 / 16}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{v_1}{v_2}=16 $$

Therefore, the ratio of terminal velocities $\frac{v_1}{v_2}$ is 16 .

Hence, the correct option is (D).
